title: Dungeon Degenerates: The Hand of Doom - The Traditional Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=24Ib0j0xDxE

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns with a more traditional five-loves, five-dislikes review structure of *The Hand of Doom*. The criticisms cut through the grimy aesthetic: keyword overload can bog down solo play, requiring constant rulebook checks; fiddly tokens demand obsessive organization; the occasionally hard-to-read map creates confusion between roads and paths, making navigation unintuitive; the punishing price tag once you add all the world books compounds the entry barrier; and sleeved components barely fit the original box. But the loves dominate: phenomenal art direction and world-building that feels ancient and established; deep player agency through branching campaign paths that feel organic rather than arbitrary; robust character development despite mechanical simplicity; the elegant balance between threat management and adventure fantasy; and absurdly polished execution for first-time game designers. Daniel's synthesis crystallizes design excellence: this game nails the marriage between form and function, proving that a small indie publisher from Portland can outpace traditionally published competitors through sheer vision and craft.

----

Have you run into the keyword/rules overload that Daniel mentions? And more importantly—does the deep campaign branching make this feel worth the price investment?
